claim.statement_location: Page 751, first paragraph of the Introduction ("In this study, we examine the factors that predict whether an employee chooses to voluntarily leave the federal service.")

data.source: 2006 Federal Human Capital Survey (FHCS) administered by the U.S. Office of Personnel Management
data.notes: Survey sample is representative of full-time, permanent U.S. federal employees on key demographic and geographic indicators.

method.description: The authors use cross-sectional survey data from the 2006 FHCS to estimate logistic regression models that relate three clusters of predictors (demographic, workplace satisfaction, and organizational/relational factors) to two dichotomous measures of turnover intention.
steps: Obtain 2006 FHCS micro-data for more than 200,000 federal employees.
Construct two dichotomous dependent variables: (a) intention to leave current agency for another federal job; (b) intention to leave the federal government for an outside job.
Create independent variables for age categories, agency tenure, race/ethnicity, multiple satisfaction measures, empowerment, performance culture perceptions, and relationship measures.
Generate interaction terms between age categories and (i) satisfaction with benefits and (ii) satisfaction with advancement.
Estimate two logistic regression models (one for each turnover-intention outcome) with robust standard errors clustered by agency.
Compute predicted probabilities and first differences using Monte-Carlo simulations with the Clarify program in Stata 11.
Interpret coefficients and simulated effects to identify key predictors of turnover intention.
method.models: Logistic regression with robust standard errors clustered by agency; post-estimation simulations (Clarify)
method.independent_variables: Age categories, agency tenure, race/ethnicity, overall job satisfaction, satisfaction with pay, satisfaction with benefits, satisfaction with advancement, performance culture index, empowerment, relationship with supervisor, relationship with coworkers, interaction terms between age and satisfaction variables
method.control_variables: Interaction terms and all listed independent variables mutually controlled in the multivariate models
method.tools_software: Stata 11 with Clarify program for simulations

results.summary: Overall job satisfaction and age show the strongest and most consistent associations with turnover intentions. Higher job satisfaction, longer tenure, and stronger supervisor relationships reduce the likelihood of intending to leave. Middle-aged employees are more inclined to move to another federal job but less inclined to exit the government altogether, while older employees are least likely to express any turnover intention. Non-white employees are more likely than whites to intend to switch agencies but less likely to intend to leave the federal service. Satisfaction with advancement opportunities substantially lowers intra-agency turnover intention, whereas a perceived performance culture slightly increases it.

metadata.original_paper_title: So Hard to Say Goodbye? Turnover Intention among U.S. Federal Employees
